data.description:
# S3 â€” OIDC / JWKS JWT verifier

## Why
JWKS-backed JWT validation is the "OAuth" half of the confirmed floor and the generic
"plug into any OIDC provider" path â€” one verifier covers AWS Cognito, Microsoft Entra ID,
Auth0, Okta, Keycloak, WorkOS, Google ID tokens, and GitHub Actions OIDC, because they all
publish a JWKS and issue standard JWTs. It is the single highest-leverage verifier for the
epic's "wide variety of environments" goal.

## What
Add a **`JWKSTokenVerifier`** to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`, registered on the composite seam and
selected via `mcp.auth.strategies` with params `jwks_uri`, `issuer`, `audience`, `algorithms`
(default `["RS256","ES256"]`), optional `leeway`. Implement with PyJWT (`jwt.PyJWKClient` +
`jwt.decode`); add `pyjwt[crypto]` to the `[mcp]` extra and pin `mcp>=1.28,<2` in `pyproject.toml`.
It validates signature (pinned algorithms), `iss`, `aud`, `exp`/`iat`/`nbf`, maps
`scope`/`scp`â†’scopes, and returns an `AccessToken` with the claims.

## Approach & rationale
Use PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + `kid` lookup + refetch-on-rotation)
and `jwt.decode` with an explicit pinned `algorithms` allowlist â€” PyJWT's documented, mainstream
JWKS path, and the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses, so we get provider-generic OIDC
validation in ~15 lines on the SDK seam. PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`; making it explicit
(`pyjwt[crypto]`) guarantees the `cryptography` backend for RS/ES verification.

## Scope
The JWKS/JWT verifier only, on the S2 seam. Not the introspection/proxy/custom verifiers.
Subset of the epic. Depends on S2.

## Security
Boundary-crossing. Hard requirements (RFC 8725 JWT BCP): **algorithm pinning** â€” reject
`alg:none` and never derive the algorithm from the token header (blocks the RS256â†”HS256
confusion attack); mandatory `aud` (RFC 8707) + `iss` + `exp` validation with bounded clock-skew
`leeway`; **SSRF-safe JWKS fetch** â€” `jwks_uri` fixed at construction, HTTPS-only, bounded
timeout, from trusted config not the token (no attacker-controlled `jku`/`x5u`); JWKS cached with
a bounded TTL and rate-limited refetch on unknown `kid` (key rotation without a refetch-DoS);
fail-closed on any error.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `pyproject.toml` adds `pyjwt[crypto]` to `[mcp]` and pins `mcp>=1.28,<2`; `make typecheck`
      resolves the imports.
- [ ] Unit test (tier: unit against the real verifier; the irreducible boundary â€” the JWKS HTTP
      fetch â€” is a stateful fake serving a locally-generated key set) asserts: a JWT signed by the
      test key with correct `iss`/`aud` â†’ an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes (observable
      oracle: returned `AccessToken` fields).
- [ ] Unit tests assert rejection (returns None) for: wrong `iss`; wrong `aud`; expired `exp`; a
      token with `alg:none`; and a token whose `alg` is `HS256` signed with the RSA public key as
      the HMAC secret (the RS/HS confusion attack) â€” each a distinct negative control.
- [ ] Unit test asserts key rotation is handled: a token signed by a newly-rotated key validates
      after the JWKS refetch, using the stateful JWKS fake (observable oracle: 2nd token validates,
      no unbounded refetch).
- [ ] Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`) asserts a valid JWKS-issued token is accepted (200)
      and an unsigned/garbage bearer is rejected (401) end-to-end through the transport.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.

## Compatibility
Additive verifier; opt-in via `mcp.auth.strategies`. The dependency pin/add is the only
cross-cutting change, backward-compatible with existing `[mcp]` installs.
data.title: S3 â€” OIDC / JWKS JWT verifier
